map.condition: Count commutative binary operations on n elements
Count ALL binary operations on n elements
Check if structure is a monoid
Check if structure is a group
Check commutativity from a Cayley table
map.use: a * b = b * a \iff \text{table is symmetric about the main diagonal}

formulas.name: Total binary operations on a set of n elements
formulas.latex: |\text{BinOps}(S)| = n^{n^2}
formulas.plain: Total binary ops = n^(n^2)
formulas.whenToUse: When asked how many distinct binary operations exist on a set of size n.

terms.means: number of elements in set S
number of cells in the n×n Cayley table

formulas.trap: Students often write n^n or 2^n. The table has n^2 cells, each independently choosing from n values, giving n^(n^2).

formulas.name: Commutative binary operations on n elements
formulas.latex: |\text{CommBinOps}(S)| = n^{n(n+1)/2}
formulas.plain: Commutative binary ops = n^(n(n+1)/2)
formulas.whenToUse: When asked to count binary operations that are commutative (a*b = b*a for all a,b).

terms.means: number of elements in set S
number of independent cells: n diagonal + C(n,2) upper-triangle entries

formulas.trap: Commutativity means the table is symmetric — only the upper triangle (including diagonal) is free. There are n + n(n-1)/2 = n(n+1)/2 free entries, each choosing from n values.

formulas.name: Associative operations (count)
formulas.latex: \text{No simple closed form; } n^{n^2} \gg |\text{AssocOps}| \gg n^{n(n+1)/2}
formulas.plain: Associative ops count has no simple closed form; fewer than all ops but no easy formula
formulas.whenToUse: When asked whether associativity has a nice counting formula — the answer is no (only special cases are known).
formulas.trap: GATE sometimes asks to verify associativity from a table — you must check (a*b)*c = a*(b*c) for all triples, NOT just a sample.

formulas.name: Commutativity via Cayley table symmetry
formulas.latex: (S,*) \text{ is commutative} \iff \text{Cayley table is symmetric about main diagonal}
formulas.plain: (S,*) is commutative <=> table[i][j] = table[j][i] for all i,j
formulas.whenToUse: When given a Cayley table and asked if the operation is commutative.

terms.means: entries where row index = column index (a*a)

formulas.trap: A table can be non-symmetric yet still commutative for specific pairs — you must check ALL off-diagonal pairs.

formulas.name: Groupoid (Magma) — Definition
formulas.latex: (S, *): \text{ closure only} \quad a*b \in S \; \forall a,b \in S
formulas.plain: Groupoid: (S, *) where a*b in S for all a,b in S. Only closure required.
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying the weakest algebraic structure — just closed under the operation.
formulas.trap: Closure is often assumed implicitly; always verify it first before checking other properties.

formulas.name: Semigroup — Definition
formulas.latex: (S, *) \text{ is a semigroup} \iff \text{Closure} + \text{Associativity}
formulas.plain: Semigroup: closed + associative. No identity or inverse required.
formulas.whenToUse: When a structure is closed and associative but may lack identity or inverses.

terms.means: (a*b)*c = a*(b*c) for all a,b,c in S

formulas.trap: Commutativity is NOT required for a semigroup. Example: matrix multiplication under 2x2 integer matrices is a semigroup but not commutative.

formulas.name: Monoid — Definition
formulas.latex: (S, *) \text{ is a monoid} \iff \text{Closure} + \text{Associativity} + \exists e \in S : e*a = a*e = a \; \forall a
formulas.plain: Monoid: semigroup + identity element e where e*a = a*e = a for all a in S.
formulas.whenToUse: Whenever checking if a semigroup has an identity — if yes, it is a monoid.
formulas.trap: The identity must satisfy BOTH e*a = a AND a*e = a. A left identity alone does not make a monoid unless the operation is commutative.

formulas.name: Group — Definition
formulas.latex: (S, *) \text{ is a group} \iff \text{Monoid} + \forall a \in S, \exists a^{-1} \in S : a * a^{-1} = a^{-1} * a = e
formulas.plain: Group: monoid where every element a has an inverse a^-1 with a*a^-1 = a^-1*a = e.
formulas.whenToUse: After confirming monoid properties, check if every element has an inverse.
formulas.trap: Inverse must be two-sided: both a*a^-1 = e AND a^-1*a = e. A left inverse alone does not guarantee a right inverse in general.

formulas.name: Abelian (Commutative) Group
formulas.latex: (S, *) \text{ is Abelian} \iff \text{Group} + a * b = b * a \; \forall a, b \in S
formulas.plain: Abelian group: group + commutativity (a*b = b*a for all a,b).
formulas.whenToUse: When asked if a group is commutative or Abelian.
formulas.trap: Not all groups are Abelian. Symmetric group S_n for n >= 3 is non-Abelian.

formulas.name: Algebraic Hierarchy (containment)
formulas.latex: \text{Abelian Group} \subset \text{Group} \subset \text{Monoid} \subset \text{Semigroup} \subset \text{Groupoid}
formulas.plain: Abelian Group ⊂ Group ⊂ Monoid ⊂ Semigroup ⊂ Groupoid
formulas.whenToUse: Quickly locating a structure in the hierarchy — every group is a monoid, every monoid is a semigroup, etc.

terms.means: every instance of the left is also an instance of the right

formulas.trap: The converse does not hold: a monoid need not be a group (may lack inverses).

formulas.name: Uniqueness of Identity Element
formulas.latex: e_1 = e_1 * e_2 = e_2 \implies e_1 = e_2
formulas.plain: If e1 and e2 are both identities, then e1 = e1*e2 = e2. Identity is unique.
formulas.whenToUse: When proving or verifying that a monoid/group has exactly one identity.
formulas.trap: Some GATE questions give a structure where a left identity exists but differs from a right identity — that structure is NOT a monoid.

formulas.name: Uniqueness of Inverse in a Group
formulas.latex: \text{In a group, } a^{-1} \text{ is unique for each } a \in S
formulas.plain: Every element in a group has a unique two-sided inverse.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked whether an element can have multiple inverses in a group.
formulas.trap: Uniqueness of inverse only holds in groups (associative + identity + inverse). In a general monoid inverses may not exist at all.

formulas.name: Cancellation Law in Groups
formulas.latex: a * b = a * c \implies b = c \quad (\text{left cancellation})
formulas.plain: In a group: a*b = a*c => b=c, and b*a = c*a => b=c.
formulas.whenToUse: When simplifying equations inside a group — you can cancel a common factor from one side.
formulas.trap: Cancellation is valid in groups but NOT in general semigroups or monoids (where inverses may not exist).

formulas.name: (Z, +) is a commutative group; (N, +) is a commutative monoid
formulas.latex: (\mathbb{Z}, +): \text{Group (identity } 0 \text{, inverse } {-a}); \quad (\mathbb{N}, +): \text{Monoid (identity } 0 \text{, no inverses for } a>0)
formulas.plain: (Z,+) is a group with identity 0 and inverse -a. (N,+) is only a monoid — positive integers have no additive inverse in N.
formulas.whenToUse: Classic example when asked about number sets under addition.
formulas.trap: Whether 0 is in N matters: with 0, (N,+) has identity; without 0, it is only a semigroup.

formulas.name: (Z, ×) is a commutative monoid, NOT a group
formulas.latex: (\mathbb{Z}, \times): \text{Monoid (identity } 1); \quad 2 \text{ has no inverse in } \mathbb{Z}
formulas.plain: (Z,×) is a commutative monoid with identity 1. Most elements have no multiplicative inverse in Z.
formulas.whenToUse: When checking if Z under multiplication forms a group — it does not.
formulas.trap: Only {1,-1} have inverses in Z under multiplication; all other integers do not.

formulas.name: String concatenation (Σ*, ·) is a monoid
formulas.latex: (\Sigma^*, \cdot): \text{Monoid; identity} = \varepsilon \text{ (empty string); associative but NOT commutative}
formulas.plain: (Sigma*, concat): monoid with identity = empty string epsilon; associative but not commutative.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked about free monoid, language theory examples, or string operations.

terms.means: set of all strings over alphabet Sigma (including empty string)

formulas.trap: String concatenation is NOT commutative ('ab' != 'ba'), so it is a monoid but not an Abelian monoid. It also is not a group since non-empty strings have no inverse.

formulas.name: Square matrices under multiplication: monoid, not group
formulas.latex: (M_{n\times n}(\mathbb{Z}), \times): \text{Monoid; identity} = I_n; \text{ non-invertible matrices have no inverse}
formulas.plain: n×n integer matrices under multiplication: monoid with identity In; non-invertible (det=0) matrices have no inverse.
formulas.whenToUse: Example of an associative, non-commutative monoid with a non-universal inverse.
formulas.trap: GL(n,Q) (invertible matrices over Q) IS a group, but M_n(Z) is not. Know which set you are working in.

formulas.name: Subtraction on Z: non-commutative AND non-associative
formulas.latex: a - b \neq b - a \text{ (non-commutative)}; \quad (a-b)-c \neq a-(b-c) \text{ (non-associative)}
formulas.plain: Subtraction: a-b != b-a and (a-b)-c != a-(b-c). Neither commutative nor associative.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked for a binary operation on Z that is neither associative nor commutative.
formulas.trap: A common GATE fill-in: subtraction is often incorrectly stated as associative. Always verify with a counterexample: (5-3)-1=1 but 5-(3-1)=3.

formulas.name: Finding identity from a Cayley table
formulas.latex: e \text{ is identity} \iff \text{row}_e = \text{header row} \text{ AND } \text{col}_e = \text{header column}
formulas.plain: e is identity if the row of e equals the top header AND the column of e equals the left header.
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying identity element from a given Cayley table.

terms.means: row in the table corresponding to e as left operand
column in the table corresponding to e as right operand

formulas.trap: Check BOTH the row and the column — a left identity only (row matches) does not mean it is also a right identity.

formulas.name: Finding inverses from a Cayley table
formulas.latex: a^{-1} = b \iff a*b = e \text{ AND } b*a = e
formulas.plain: b is the inverse of a if a*b = e and b*a = e (both directions).
formulas.whenToUse: Checking whether every element has an inverse (determining if a monoid is a group).
formulas.trap: In a non-commutative table, a*b = e does NOT guarantee b*a = e — always verify both entries.
